Imagine that the fuel quantity gauges are showing a total of 50 GALS on board, and the "total fuel used" gauge is showing 27.2 GALS used over the past 2 HRS and 17 MINS. At departure, you had 86 GALS on board. You have a fuel leak between the tank and the fuel flow sensor that is causing the difference, and you want to know if you'll have enough fuel to get to the more operationally favorable diversion airport, rather than the closest in point of time. To get to the operationally favorable diversion airport you will burn 13 GALS cruise, descent, and landing, and those steps will require 48 MINS. You'd like to land with at least 6 GALS on board as a safe reserve. If you assume that the leak rate has been constant since takeoff, and divert now, how much fuel will you have on landing at the operationally favorable diversion airport?

In the last question, we assumed that the fuel leak rate had been constant since takeoff. Unfortunately, this was not the case. On the way to the operationally favorable diversion airport at 2300' AGL, your only engine has stopped producing power. Your fuel flow gauge shows zero GALS per hour. Your fuel quantity gauge shows zero on both sides. How did you not catch this sooner? There is an airport 5.4 NM away and the average glide angle to the airport will yield 400'/NM. Do you have sufficient energy to make it to that airport? Choose the answer that best describes your situation.
choice_a: No, you will be .3 NM short.
choice_b: No, your altitude at the airport would have been -320' AGL (BGL?).
choice_c: Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 140' AGL.
choice_d: Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 340' AGL.
correct_choice: Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 140' AGL.
